MERELY
MENTIONED
The Liquor Dealers’ Association is in
session this afternoon and Mayor Fa
gan’s political move to raise the license
fee from $250 to $500 is the topic of
discussion. That the liquor dealers feel
they are being unjustly treated by the
administration wag evident from the con
versation of many members before the
meeting. A determined stand will be
taken to defeat the proposed increase.
The liquor dealers fear that if the li
cense is raised by Mayor Fagan’s retir
ing board) it will remain at the $500 fig
ure for many years to come, as no future
board would care to risk public disap
proval by reducing the rate.'
From information received by the
liquor dealers the Mayor is fully bent on
having the fee raised. If hie board balks
on the proposition, some of hie friends
say he will hold up all licenses until the
new Excise Board takes hold and force
that body to go on record for or against
the proposed increase.
There was a hot old time last night at
the meeting of the Mark M. Fagan As
sociation of Hudson County. Forest
Heath, the Fagan leader of the Tenta
Ward, whose followers are in the ma
jority in the club, started the trouble by
having introduced a resolution endorsing
the candidacy of Thomas Durkin for the
Second Criminal Court Clerkship. Dur
kin has many enemies among the non
office holders and they fought the resolu
tion tooth and nail. Heath had the ma
jority at his back and the resolution pre
vailed.
During the discussion the Reformers
acted like ordinary politicians. “Liar,”
traitor,” sneak,” and other choice words
were hurled at each other. “Pete” Don
nellan, whom Heath doesn’t like because
he has red hair, got into a dispute with
“Bill’ Whitely, Heath’s candidate for
Freeholder in the Tenth Ward, and be
fore they knew it they were in to cacn
other’s wool. After each had landed! a
few uppercuts they were separated.
Much ill-feeling developed during the
meeting and the members who do not
like Heath’s leadership are thinking of
withdrawing from the club and organiz
ing a club of their own.
While the Faganites were scrapping
among themselves at the meeting of the
Fagan Association the Republicans who
stand for Col. Dickinson and the party
organization were having a love feast
in the Fifth Ward, the stamping ground
of Mayor Fagan and City Hall Custo
dian Connolly, who looks after the prac
tical end of the Mayor’s political game.
The organisation Republicans are grow
ing stronger every day in the Fifth and
they are confident they will defeat the
Faganites at the primaries.
Herman Berg, who, because George L.
Record patted him on the back for some
anti-organization speeches he made at
Trenton last winter during the legisla
tive session thinks he is a statesman,
has changed his mind about running for
Congress in the Tenth District. He
knew he would) stand no chance against
“Jimmy” Hamill. He thinks he would
have an easier time against C< ugressman
Van Winkle, and he will try and carry
the primaries in the Ninth District.
Berg will come to his senses long before
the date of the primaries.

PLEA FORCHINESE
Chauncey R. Burr of Port
land Maine Makes a Vig
orous Defense of the Ce
lestial
HE IS MISUNDERSTOOD
SENATOR HALE PRESENTS THE
DEFENCE MS A SENATE DOCU
MENT AND IT CONTAINS SOME
USEFUL INFORMATION.
WASHINGTON, June 5, 1906.—It is
so much the rule to take for granted, the
idea that we don’t want the Chinese in
this country that it is refreshing and in
teresting to have somebody get up in
meeting and talk on the other side, as
Ohauncey R. Burr, of Portland1, Me., has
done in a statement just published by
Senator Hale as a senate document.
Mr. Burr thinks we don’t understand and
appreciate the Chinese, and especially the
Chinese coolie, in this country. He says
we have got to have a new- definition of
our relations to China. It is our gra
tuitous insult to the Chinese as a nation,
and the mean treatment we have accord
ed Chinese students and other intelligent
Chinese visiting this country, he says,
that is responsible for tlie Chinese boy
cott of American goods.
“Now, "that the question of Chinese ex
clusion and the offensive way in which it
has been practised has been brought to
the attention of the Chinese people as a
whole,” says Mr. Bun-, “it will certainly
not be allowed to drop till the ethical
question involved has been thoroughly
ventilated, viz., why the Chinese laborer
is uot wanted in the United States at all.
“China is finding herself through much
tribulation. A national spirit is growing,
and it is with this that, the United States
has suddenly come face to face. An of
fended nation, and an angry nation of the
size of China may suddtenly blossom into
a world power. The example of Japan
has not been lost on her or no her nation
al spirit It behooves us, therefore, to
treat her seriously and to stop talking
in our public prints of ‘John Chinaman,’
‘the heathen Chinee,’ ‘chinks’ and ‘Celes
tials.’ This- is not the way friends are
made.”
Even if we don’t want the Chinese
coolie in this country, Mr. Burr thinks,
we do want him and need' him in the
Philippines and' in Hawaii, and above
all. he suggests, we want China’s- trade.
If we don’t get it we might as well, he
contends, give up work on the Panama
Canal, as outside of China the Eastern
trade is not worth much. Mr. Burr de
fends the Chinese merchant as honesty
personified, the. Chinese workman as
industrious, efficient ami diligent, the
Chinese cook as the equal of the French
chef. The coolie, he declares, is temper
ate and frugal. He admits that the
Chinese, some of them, anyway, like
opium, but says that most of them use
it iu moderation, and doesn’t think they
are worse or more numerous, proportion
ately, than the cocaine fiends and drunk
ards of America.
“He is said to be vicious and of im
moral life,” says Mr. Burr. “Are other
nations so saintly that they can afford to
throw stones? Does he take money out
of the country? Sb do Italians' and
plenty of others. Finally, he is said to
stand aloof and not merge his individ
uality with that of the American people.
He is an alien always. This is true, and1
probably always will be. But it is also
true of tlie negro. None of these objec
tions is worth being repeated a second
time. Tlie real objection lies deeper. It
is not often heard; perhaps it is not often
formulated. Our workingmen feel able
to cope with all other nationalities on
equal or superior term®, but they are
afraid of the coolie, afraid of his efficien
cy, his tireless industry, his temperance,
his frugality.
‘Do we want the coolie in the United
States? The great mass of the people
are indifferent to the question. He has
not come within the horizon of their ex
perience, They know little about him,
and core less. It is otherwise, however,
with the labor unions. They give a nega
tive answer in no uncertain tone, and
they are backed up in this negation by
the newspapers which cater to the labor
vote. Better to lose the Oriental trade
alogether, says this faction, than to ad
mit tie coolie at all to competition with
our workman.
.“On the other hand stands the Nation
al Association of Manufacturers, with its
membership at 3,500 memebers, employ
ing 500,000 working people and $800,
000,000 of capital. It sees its industry
cripled, its capital threatened by the
Chinese boycott, and will naturally uise
every means in its power to avert the im
FAGAN CLUB MEN
COME TO BLOWS
Difference of Opinion As to
the Merits of Candidates
for Court Clerk Ends In
Fisticu ffs
FIFTY FIGHTERS IN THE FIELD
MEETING ADJOURNED AMID THE
ROW, BUT WHEN IT HAD END
ED BUSINESS WAS RESUMED
AND THOMAS DURKIN WAS EN
DORSED
There was a heated argument at last
night’s meeting of the Mark Fagan Asso
ciation. It ended in a pretty general
melee. The trouble arose over the in
dorsement of a candidate for clerk of
■the Second Criminal Court. Two mem
bers of the association wanted to be in
dorsed—Thomas Durkin, who had the
support of the members who hold offices;
and Peter Donnelan, whose supporters
are those members of the club who do
not hold office.
William Whitley, one of Durkin’s
warmest supporters, and an employee at
the City Hall, made a speech in which
he roundly criticised the opposition and
made a few sharp references to Donne
lan. As he took his seat Donnelan went
up to him and demanded to know why
he had made such a vigorous attack.
Whiteiy told him to be quiet. Donnelan,
throwing off his coat, dealt Whiteiy a
stinging blow, and in a minute they were
rolling over the floor.
The chairman adjourned the meeting
while half a hundred members engaged
in a fight. When they had had enough
the meeting was resumed and Durkin
was indorsed by a large majority.
pending catastrophe. These men are
broad-minded citizens, who are out in the
world’s markets to win., wTho recognize
the absurdity of demanding an open door
in China and a closed one at home, and
are willing to give a Roland for an Oli
ver. .
“The Chinese exclusion laws have got
to be modified, and it is for Congress to
decide how.
“If the coolie is really such a terrible
fellow that the American workingman
cannot meet him on equal terms, then
let him on unequal terms. Limit the
nember admitted in any one year or ad
mit only skilled artisans or those with a
property qualification. Forbid his buying
land in this country and make it impos
sible for him to become a citizen. If the
American workingham is afraid to meet
him on these conditioins he is a coward
indeed.
“But a far more practical way, and
one which ■would arouse little or no re
sentment anywhere, is to open the doors
freely to him in onr insular possessions.
The 'Sandlwich Islands, Guam, the Phil
ippines, Porto Rico, all need him. Effi
cient, tireless labor is scarce in all these
places and commands good prices. The
Philippines, especially, need the Chinese
merchant, artisan and coolie. A residence
of three hundred' years there has endear
ed the islands to them. They lie just
off the Chinese coast. With all the en
couragement to immigration given them
by the Spanish Government, who knew
their worth, their numbers never exceed
ed 41,000.
“Her,e then, is a practical answer to
our difficulties. Open the doors of the
United States conditionally to a limited
number of coolies and the doors of our
colonies unconditionally. The Philippines
are Oriental ground1 and it is no shock
to the feelings of its inhabitants to ad
mit other Orientals.
“We shall thus gain the good will of
the Chinese people, or at least cease to
feel their hostility and be able to cope
with other na twins on equal footing in
their struggle for htat prize of the Ori
ent the China trade.”

GOVERNOR WILL
REPLY TO KIISER
He Does Not Propose a Con
troversy As to Veraoity
But Will State His Posi
tion On tbe Dryden Boom
MORGAN BASKS THE SENATOR
SO DOES STATE CHAIRMAN
FRANK O. BRIGGS—COLONEL
DICKINSON IS NON-COMMITTAL
(Special to “The Jersey City News.”)
TRENTON, June 6, 1906.—The chief
tepis discussed at the State House yes
terday was the United States Senatorial
situation as intensified1 by Senator John
F. Dryden’s announcement of his candi
dacy for renomination, made by his son
in-law, Colonel Anthony R. Kuser, yes
terday.
It was not the fact that the Senator
had expressed his determination to seek
a re-election that created the discussion,
as that was expected1, and to some extent
discounted. It was, however, the positive
and firm manner in which Colonel Kuser
handed out the names of the Governor,
Chairman Frank O. Briggs, of the Re
publican State committee and other lead
ers of the party as those of men pledged
in unmistakable terms to the Senator’s
re-election.
Particularly fruitful in speculation
was the statement attributed to Gover
nor Stokes. According to Colonel Kuser
the Governor was one of the first to
pledge his suport to Mr. Dryden, “and
later, when it was intimated that he was >
a tentative candidate for the office him
self, Governor Stokes again assured' me
positively that he was for Mr. Dryden,
and would not be a candidate against
him.”
Everybody was anxious to hear what
the Governor would have to say in re
gard to this positive statement of Colonel
Kuser’s, and when he arrived at the
house much earlier than usual, several
of his friends thought, he looked as if he
had something in his mind. A corre
spondent ^showed him, .C.olonel Kuser’s
statement regarding his candidacy.
The Governor, with a deprecating
smile, said:—
“Why, I thought I had made enough
statements on that subject.”
When asked if the colonel was exactly
correct in quoting him as having said
that he would not be a candidate against
Mr. Dryden, the Governor said:—
“No; I do not propose to get into a
controversy with Colonel Kuser on the
subject of veracity, but I will prepare a
statement on the subject and give it to
you later.”
The Governor then hurried into his
private office and tackled the business
, . n
awaiting hirm
Comptroller J. Willard Morgan said
that as far as he was concerned, Colonel
Kuser’s statement was correct. He was
one of those who believed Senator Dry
den was entitled to a re-election and he
■was with him without equivocation. The
prevailing sentiment in Camden, and the
part of South Jersey in that vicinity he
also believed, favored Mr. Dryden’s re
election.
As to the Governor's candidacy, Mr.
Morgan said he did not believe the Chief
Executive was a candidate for United
States Senator in the strict sense of the
term, but if it should appear when the
time came to elect the Senator,that Mr.
Dryden from any cause could not make
the nomination, he saw no reason1 why
the Governor should not go in and win
if he could. It was a laudable ambition
for anyone to entertain, that of being
United States Senator, and no one doubt
ed the Governor's ability to fill the po
sition creditably to the State.
Aside from declaring that at this time
he was committed to no man for the
United States Senator, Colonel Samuel
D. Dickinson, Secretary of State, was
disinclined to discuss the situation or
Senator Dryden’s announcement of his
eandidacy through Colonel Kuser. This
method of announcing one’s candidacy
for United States Senator, he said, was
Southern and Democratic, but if it was
to be the style of campaign to be waged
here he supposed it was proper for the
Senator to announce his candidacy.
State Chairman Frank O. Briggs prac
tically admitted that as far as he was
concerned Colonel Kuser's statement was
correct. Speaking in his individual ca
pacity, and not as a State official or as
an official of the State Committee, and
he particularly emphasized that point, he
said he would say that it had always
been the custom of the Republican party
to give its United States Senators a
second term, unless there was serious
objection to them. “Senator Dryden,”
he continued, “had not even had a full
term yet, and as there is not serious
objection to him, I personally favor his
re-election to the United States Senate.
That is all I have to say on the sub
ject.”
CIVIL SUITS FOR
PASSAIC STEEL
Method By Which It Was
Financed Likely to Be
Aired In Court
PR08PECT OF RECEIVERSHIP
PLAINTIFFS HAIL FROM PATER
SON AND PENNSYLVANIA—DIS
TINGUISHED LAWYERS RE
TAINED
!
(Special to “The Jersey City News.“i
PATERIS'ON', June 6, 1906.—Ar a re
suit of the modus operandi by which the
Passaic Steel Company was financed,
there threatens to be a large number of
civil suits instituted against those back
of the scheme at that time. These suits
will probably be started as soon as a re
ceivership is applied for by the special
committee, which will be named within
a few day's. In fact, this committee
may be named' to-day, and they will de
cide upon the future of the company
some time this week, making their reply
to the stock and bond holders some time
next week.
These suits, according to information
secured last evening, will be instituted
against at least two local citizens and
a financing company of New York. They
will be asked' to restore to persons who
purchased bonds of the company, not on
ly the face value of these bonds but they
will be asked to restore to the company
certain other funds which they now hold.
The plaintiffs in these suits will be a
large number of Patersonians, as well
as many others, some of whom are resi
dents of Pennsylvania, the instigator of
the suits being a resident of Scranton,
known to nearly every bondholder in
the company and an official of a steel
company not many miles from his home.
This man has placed the matter in the
hands of his counsel and papers are now
being prepared.
Among the lawyers named who will
handle cases against the men back of
the organizing of the Passaic Steel Com
pany and the absorption of the Passaic
Rolling Mill Comapny are Prosecutor
Eugene Emley, William B. Gouriey and
Wayne Dumont.
The other side has not been standing
idle, and has also consulted counsel. It
is believed from information secured last
evening that Messrs. Griggs and Harding
and Michael and Charles Dunn have
been engaged to fight any civil actions
that may be brought against those re
ferred ro above.

FAMILIES OUT IN
NIGHT CLOTHES
Fire On Washington Street
Early This Morning Emp
ties Two Houses
PROPERTY OF E. F. C. YOUN8
OCCUPANTS WERE ALL COLOR
ED AND THEY LOST MOST OP
THEIR CLOTHES AND HOUSE
HOLD EFFECTS
A fire that did considerable damage to
property and drove more than half a
dozen occupants of two houses intVth*
street in their night clothes, broke out
on the second floor of No. 250 Washing
ton street at 1.40 o’clock this morning.
This floor was occupied by a colored
club run by Tom Ridley. It is located
over Laird’s drugstore. The building is
a three-story-and-a-half brick and frame
structure. The flames spread to the two
story frame structure at York and
Washington street, the first floor of
which is occupied by Otto Schmoidt as
a saloon and whose living apartments
are over the saloon. Both houses are
owned by E. F. C. Young, president of
the First National Bank. The two up
per floors of No. 250 are occupied by
Mrs. Ellen Richardson, her son, John
Williams, and his wife, and her grand
daughter, Mamie Hill—all colored.
Three colored lodgers occupied1 the toy
floor. They were James Frances, Josh
ua Brown and Mrs. Brooks. John Will
iam has been ill for two months and1 has
been under the care of Dr. Rector. He
had to be helped down from the third
floor and was taken to the home of a
neighbor much the worse for the shock
he received. Several members or th«
family, including the aged Mrs. Richard
son and most of the other occupants, es
caped eu deshabille. Nearly all their
furniture and clothes were burned.
Saloon-keeper 'Schmoidt and his wife
were also obliged to make their escape
to the street without taking time to
dress. His living apartments were gut
ted.. His loss, he says, is about $2,000,
hut half that amount is covered by in
surance. Mr. Schmoidt intended yester
day to raise the amount of his insurance
and is to-day kicking himself because he
neglected to do so. His saloon was
slightly damaged by water.
The ceiling of Laird’s drug store was
slightly damaged by water that leaked
through from the floor of the club room
above.
It is not known how the tire originat
ed. It was first discovered by the aged
Mrs. Richardson, who was watching over
her sick son's beside, and who was arous
ed first by the smell of smoke. She
aroused all the other inmates, a number
of whom might have been burned had it
not been for the old lady's devotion to
her son. Firemen with apparatus sooi*
responded to an alarm sent in from Box
No. 125 by a citizen.
Laird’s drug store, which escaped the
ravages of the flames, was but recently
damaged considerably by tile explosion
of some chemicals in a rear room.
The citizen who turned in the alarm
was Martin Fayliey.. proprietor of the
cafe on the northwest corner of York
and Washington streets. He and Sa
loonkeeper Sheehan and a letter carrier
were standiing in front of Fahey’s’cafe
when they espied flames creeping from
| the side of the upper story of No. 250.
In promptly sending in an alarm. Mr.
j Fahey probably saved several lives.

text:
The Exploits of Elaine
A Detective Novel and a Motion Picture Drama
Presented in Collaboration With the Pathe Players and the Eclectic Film Company
Copyright, 1914, by the Star Company All Foreign Bights Reserved
SYNOPSIS.
The New York police are mystified by a
series of murders and other crimes. The
principal clue to the criminal is the warn
ing l6tter which is sent the victims, signed
with a “clutching hand.” The latest vic
tim of the mysterious assassin is Taylor
Dodge, the insurance president. His
daughter, Elaine, employs Craig Kennedy,
the famous scientific detective, to try to
unravel the mystery. What Kennedy ac
complishes is told by his friend Jameson,
a newspaper man. After many fruitless
attempts to put Elaine and Craig Ken
nedy out of the way the Clutching Hand
is at last found to be none other than
Perry Bennett, Elaine’s lawyer and the
man she is engaged to marry. Bennett
flees to the den of one of his Chinese
criminals. The Chinaman forces from
Bennett the secret of the whereabouts of
$7,000,000. Then he gives the lawyer a
potion which will suspend animation for
months. Kennedy reaches Bennett’s side
just after he has lost consciousness.
EIGHTEENTH EPISODE
THE VENGEANCE OF WU FANG.
Elaine was still in the power of Wu
Fang. Kennedy had thwarted the
Chinese master criminal in his search
for the millions amassed by the
Clutching Hand. But any joy that he
might have derived from this success
wa3 completely obscured by the fear
that Wu might wreak some diabolical
vengeance on Elaine.
Nor were Kennedy’s fears unfounded.
Wu and Long Sin had scarcely reach
ed the secret apartment when the
subtle Chinaman began to contem
plate revenge.
Long Sin was smoking a Chinese
pipe, resting after their humed flight,
while Wu, the tireless, was seated at
a table at the other end of the room.
At last Wu Fang took up a long Chi
nese dirk from the table before him,
looked at it, turned It over, felt its
edge. He rose and deliberately walked
across to a door leading into a back
room.
On a couch lay Elaine, and with her,
as a guardian, was Weepy Mary.
As Wu, the Serpent, entered and
advanced slowly toward Elaine, she
crouched hack from him in deadly
fear.
Slowly he drew from under his robe
the Chinese dirk. He felt the edge of
it again and gazed significantly at
Elaine.
It was a critical moment.
Juiit then Lorig Sin entered. “One
of the five millions waits outside,” he
reported simply, with a bow.
Wu understood. It had been a pleas
ant fiction of his that although he did
not, of course, absolutely control such
a stupendous organization, he could,
by his subtle power, force almost un
limited allegiance from the simple
coolies in that district of China from
which he came.
Out in the front room, just a mo
ment before, a knock at the door had
disturbed Long Sin, and a Chinese
servant had announced a visitor. Long
Sin had waved to the servant to usher
him in, and a poorly clad coolie had
entered.
He bowed as Long Sin faced him.
“Where is the master?” he had asked.
Long Sin had not deigned to speak.
With a mere wave of his hand he in
dicated that he would be the bearer
of the message, and had followed Wu
through the door of the back room.
So, almost by chance, Wu was inter
rupted in the brutal vengeance which
had first come to his mind. He
sheathed the knife and, still without
a word, went back into the main room,
giving a nod to Weepy Mary to guard
Elaine closely.
Wu eyed the eoolla until the new
comer could almost feel the master’s
penetrating gaze, although his head
was bowed in awe. Quickly the coolie
thrust his hand under his blouse and
drew forth a package. With another
bow he advanced.
“For your enemies, O master,” he
said, handing the package over to
Wu.
The coolie started to open the pack
age, removed the paper wrapper, and
then a silk wrapping inside. Finally
he came to a box, from which he drew
a leather pouch, each operation con
ducted with greater care as it became
evident that the contents were espe
cially precious in some way. Then he
took from the pouch a small vial.
‘‘What Is it?” demanded Wu Fang,
as the coolie displayed it.
The coolie draw forth now a magni
fying glass and a glass slide. Opening
the vial with great care he shook
something out on the slide, then
placed it under the lens.
“Look!” he said simply.
Wu bent over and looked. Under
the lens what had formerly seemed to
be merely a black speck of dirt be
came now one of the most weird and
uncanny little creatures to be found in
all the realm of nature. It seemed to
be all legs and feelers moving at once.
A. normal person wculd have looked
at the creature only with the greatest
repugnance. Wu regarded it with a
sort of unholy fascination.
“And it is?" he queried. *
“What the white man calls the Afri
can tick which carries the recurrent
f?ver,” answered the coolie deferen
: util—-
A dash of intense exultation seemed
--V 'n Wu Fang's sinister face.
• ♦.-It, ’ ho said to the coolie, as be
By ARTHUR B. REEVE
The Well-Known Novelist and the
Creator of the "Craig Kennedy” Stories
moved slowly again into the back
room.
Long Sin had remained there. With
Weepy Mary he was guarding Elaine,
when Wu Fang re-entered. Elaine
was thoroughly aroused by this time.
“Suffering is a state of mind,” Wu
said in a low tone, “and I have de
cided* that it would be poor revenge
for me to harm you. You are free.”
Nothing could have come as a great
er surprise to Elaine.
“Do you—do you really mean it?”
she asked, scarcely able to believe
what her ears heard.
Wu merely nodded, and with a wave
of his hand to Long Sin indicated that
Elaine was to be released.
She nerved herself to take several
steps toward the doer. She had
scarcely crossed half the room.
“Wait!” ordered Wu sharply.
Was he merely torturing her, as a,
cat might torture a mouse? She
stopped obediently, afraid to look at
him.
“This will be the vengeance of Wu
Fang,” he went on impressively.
“Slowly, one by one, your friends will
weaken and die, then your family, un
til finally only you are left. Then will
come your turn.”
He stopped again and raised his
long, lean forefinger. “Go,” her hissed.
“I wish you much joy.”
* **■**• •
I don’t think that in all our friend
ship I have ever seen Kennedy so ut
terly depressed as he was when we
returned after the discovery of the
vast fortune which Bennett had clev
erly secreted.
There seemed to be absolutely noth
ing to do until some new clue turned
up. I placed my hand on his shoulder,
but the words that would encourage
him died on my lips. Several times I
started to speak, but each time I
checked myself. There did not seem
to he anything that would be appro
priate for such an occasion.
A sharp ring at the telephone made
both of ua fairly jump, so nervous
had we become. Kennedy reached
over instantly for the instrument in
the vague hope that at last there was
some news.
As I watched his face it changed
first from despair to wonder, and final
ly i% seemed to light ujo with the most
remarkable look of relief and happi
ness that one could imagine.
“I shall be right over,” he cried,
jamming the receiver on the
hook, and in the same motion reach
ing for his hat and coat. “Walter,”
he cried, “it is Elaine! They have let
her go!”
I seized my own hat and coat in
time to follow him, and we dashed out
of the laboratory.
The suspense under which Aunt
Josephine had been living had told on
He Felt the Edge of the Dagger and
Gazed Significantly at Elaine.
her. Her niece, Elaine’s oousin, Mary
Brown, who lived at Rockledge, had
come into the city to comfort Aunt
Josephine, and they had been sitting
that morning in the library.
Suddenly a startled cry from Jen
nings out in the hall caused both la
dies to jump to their feet. They could
scarcely believe what they heard as
the faithful old butler cried out the
name.
“Why, Miss Elaine!” he gasped.
An instant later Elaine herself burst
into the room and flung herself Into
Aunt Josephine’s arms. All talking
and half crying from joy at once, they
crowded about her.
In the excitement Aunt Josephine
had seized the telephone and called
our number. She did not even wait to
break the good news, but handed the
telephone to Elaine herself.
We left the laboratory on the run,
too fast to notice that just around the
building line at the corner stood a lim
ousine with shades drawn. Even if
we bad paused to glance back we
could not have seen Wu Fang and
Long Sin inside gazing out through
the corner of the curtains. They were
in European dress now, and had evi
dently come prepared for just what
they knew was likely to happen.
We were admitted by the faithful
Jennings and almost ran into the li
brary. v
“Oh, Craig!" cried Elaine, as Ken
nedy, almost speechless, seized her by
both hands.
For a few seconds none of us could
speak. Then followed a veritable flood
of eager conversation.
“Won’t yon come and stay with
me a few days up in the country,
dear?” urged Mary at last.
Elaine thought a moment, then
turned to Aunt Josephine.
“Yes,” considered her aunt, “I think
it would do you good.”
Still she hesitated, then shyly looked
at Kennedy and laughed. “You, too,
Craig, must be fagged out,” she said,
frankly. “Come up there with us and
take a rest.”
Kennedy smiled. “I shall be de
lighted,” he accepted promptly.
We chatted, then left a few mo
ments later so that Kennedy could
pack.
*******
Around the corner from the labora
tory, as we dashed out, had been, as
I have said, Wu Fang and Long Sin
looking out from the limousine. No
sooner had we disappeared across the
campus than their driver started up
ti e car and they sped around to our
apartment.
Cautiously they alighted and walked
down the street. Then, making sure
they were not observed, they entered
and mounted the stairs to our door
way. Long Sin was stationed down
the hall on guard, while Wu Fang drew
from his pocket a blank key, a file and
a candle. He lighted the candle and
held the key in its flame until it was
covered with soot.
Then he inserted the key into the
keyhole, turned it and took the key
out. He filed the key, trying it again
and again. Finally he finished and
opened the door. Beckoning Long Sin,
he entered our room.
As they stood there Wu Fang gazed
about our living room keenly. He was
evidently considering where to place
something.
Finally his eye rested on the tele
phone. It seemed to suggest an idea
to him and he crossed over to it. Care
fully holding down the receiver on the
hook, he unscrewed the case which
holds the diaphragm, while with his
clever fingers he held the rest of the
instrument intact. Then he removed
from his pocket the vial which the
coolie had given him and placed its
contents on the diaphragm itself.
Quickly now he replaced the receiver
and, having finished their work, Long
Sin and Wu Fang stealthily crept out.
A second time, as we approached
our apartment after the visit to Elaine,
Ve were too excited to notice 'the
limousine in which were Wu and Long
Sin. But no sooner had we entered
than Long Sin left the car with a
final word of instruction from his
master.
Upstairs, in the apartment, Kennedy
began hurriedly to pack, and I helped
him as well as I could. We were in
the midst of it when the telephone
rang and I answered it.
“Hello!” I called.
There was no response.
“Hello, hello!” I repeated, raising
my voice.
Still there was no answer. I worked
the hook up and down, but could get
no reply, and finally, disgusted, hung
up.
A moment later, I recall now, it
seemed to me as though someone had
stuck a pin into the lobe of my ear.
We had scarcely got back to work
when the telephone bell jangled again,
and a second time I answered it.
“Is Mr. Kennedy there?” came back
a strange voice.
I handed the instrument to Craig.
“Hello!” he called. “Who is this?”
No response.
iHello, hello!” he shouted, working
the hook as I had done and, in my
case, there was still no answer.
Neither of us thought anything of
it at the time, but now I recall that I
did see Kennedy once or twice press
the lobe of his ear as though some
thing had hurt it.
We did not know until later that in
a pay station down the street our
arch enemy, Long Sin, had been call
ing us up and then, with a wicked
smile, refusing to speak to us.
• *••*••
It w'as about a week later that I
came home late one night from the
Star, feeling pretty done up. What
ever it was, a violent fever seemed
to have come on me suddenly. The
next morning when I woke up I felt
a lassitude that made it quite hard
enough even to lounge about in my
bathrobe. Finally, feeling no better,
I decided to see a doctor. I put on
my clothes with a decided effort and
went out.
The nearest doctor was about half
a block away and we scarcely knew
him, for neither Kennedy nor I are
exactly sickly.
He sounded me and pounded me,
talked a little while on generalities,
diet and exercise, then walked over
to a cabinet, and emptied out a few
pills into a little paper box.
“Take one every hour,” he said.
“Cut your cigarettes to three a day,
and don’t drink coffee. Four dollars,
please.’
I paid him and went back to the
apartment, my head soon in a whirl
from anew onset of the fever.
I managed to get back into my bath
robe, and threw myself down on the
divan, propped up with pillows. I had
taken the pills, but they had no more
affect than sugar or milk. By this
THE ODANAH STAR
time I was much more delirious and
was crying out.
I saw faces about me, but I did not
see the faces which were actually out
by our hall door. Wu Fang and Long
Sin had waited patiently for their re
venge. Now that they thought suffi
cient time had elapsed, they had sto
len silently to the apartment door.
While Ling Sin watched, Wu listened.
“The white devil has it,” whispered
Wu Fang, as he rejoined his fellow
conspirator.
How long I should have remained
in this state, and in fact how long I
did remain, I don’t know. Vaguely, I
recall that our acquaintance, Johnson,
who had the apartment across the
hall, at last heard my cries and came
in.
Somehow or other I knew the state
I was in. I knew it was Johnson, yet
it all seemed unreal to me. With a
great effort I gathered all my scat
tered wits and managed to shout out,
“‘Telegraph Kennedy—Rockledge.”
*******
At about the same time, up at Rock
ledge, Kennedy and Elaine, with her
cousin, Mary Brown, were starting out
for a horseback ride through the hills.
They were chatting gayly, but Ken
nedy was forcing himself to do so.
In fact, they had scarcely gone half
a mile when Kennedy, who was rid
ing between the two and fighting off
by sheer nerve the illness he felt, sud
denly fell over in half a faint on the

Jx&2>s ■■ ' % 1%. ','\
horse’s neck. Elaine and Mary reined
up their horses.
“Why, Craig,” cried Elaine, startled,
“what’s the matter?”
The sound of her voice seemed to
arouse him. Ee braced up. “Oh, noth
ing, I guess,” he said with‘a forced
smile. “I’m all right.”
It was no use, however. They had
to cut short the ride, and Kennedy re
turned to the house glad to drop down
in an easy chair on the porch, while
Elaine hovered about him solicitously.
They summoned the country doctor,
but it took him some time to get out
to the house. Suddenly a messenger
boy rode up on his bicycle and mount
ed the porch steps. “Telegram for
Mr. Kennedy,” he announced, looking
about and picking out Craig naturally
as the person he wanted.
Kennedy nodded and took the yel
low envelope while Elaine signed for
it. Listlessly he tore it open. It
read:
Craig Kennedy,
Care Wellington Brown,
Rockledge, N. Y.
Jameson very ill. Wants you. Better
come. Johnson.
The message seemed to rouse Ken
nedy in spite of his fever. His face
showed keen alarm, which he endeav
orded to conceal from Elaine. But her
quick eye had caught the look.
“I must see Walter,” he exclaimed,
rising rather, weakly and going into
the house.
How he ever did it is still, I think, a
mystery to him, but he managed to
pack up and, in spite of the alternating
fever and chills, make the journey
back to the city.
When at last Craig arrived at our
apartment, it must have seemed to
him that he found me almost at
death’s door.
11l himself, Kennedy threw himself
down for a moment, exhausted. “When
did this thing come on, Walter?” he
asked of Johnson.
“Yesterday, I think, as nearly as I
can find out,” replied our friend.
Craig was decidedly worried.
“There’s only one person in New York
to call on,” he murmured, pulling him
self out of bed and getting into the
living room as best he could.
“Is that you, Godowski?’ he asked
over the telephone. “Well, doctor,
this is Kennedy. Come over to my
apartment, quick. I’ve a case —two
cases, for you.”
Godowski was a world-famous scien
tist in his line and had specialized in
bacteriology, mainly in tropical dis
eases.
As Kennedy hung up the receiver
he made his way back again to the
bedroom, scratching his ear. He no
ticed that I was doing the same in
my delirium.
“Has Walter been scratching his
ear?” he asked of Johnson.
Johneson nodded. “That’s strange,”
considered Craig, thoughtfully. “I’ve
been doing the same.”
He turned back into the living room
and for a moment looked about Fi
nally his eye happened to fall on the
telephone and an idea seemed to oc
cur to him.
He went over to the instrument and
unscrewed the receiver. Carefully he
“Look!’* Said Kennedy, Turning to His Microscope.
looked inside. Then he looked closer.
Ihere was something peculiar about it
and he picked up a blank sheet of
white paper, dusting off the diphragm
on it. There, on the paper, were in
numerable little black specks.
Just then, outside, Doctor Godow
ski’s car drew up and he jumped out,
swinging his black bag. Not being ac
quainted with what we were going
through, Godowski did not notice the
almond-eyed Chinaman w„o was
watching down the street.
“How do you do, doctor?” greeted
Craig faintly, at the door.
“What seems to be the difficulty?”
inquired the doctor eagerly.
“I don’t know,” returned Craig,
“but I have my suspicions. I’m too ill
to verify them myself, so I’ve called
on you. Look at Jameson first,” he
added.
While Godowski was examining me,
Craig managed to get out his micro
scope and was looking through it at
the strange black specks on the pa
per. There, under the lens, he could
see the most remarkable, almost mi
croscopic creature, all legs and feel
ers, a. most vicious object.
Weak though he was, he could not
help an exclamation of exultation at
his discovery, just as Godowski had
finished with me.
“Look!” he cried, calling the doctor.
“I know what the trouble is, Godow
ski.”
He had started to tell, but the ex-
citemant of the journey and the exer
tion were so great that he could hard
ly mumble.
“Here —look —on this paper,” he
cried. “From the telephone—”
He had risen and was handing the
paper to the scientist when his weak
ness overcame him. He fell flat on his
face on the floor and dropped the pa
per, spilling the contents.
Godowski, now thoroughly alarmed,
bent over Craig. But the delirium
had overcome Kennedy, too.
Unable to make any sense out of
Craig’s broken wanderings, Godowski
lost no time in taking samples of our
blood.
Then he hurried away to his labora
tory in his car. As he did so, however,
Long Sin leaped into a taxicab which
was waiting, and followed.
*******
In Godowski’s laboratory, where he
was studying tropical diseases, the
bacteriologist set to work at once to
confirm his own growing suspicions.
From a monkey which he had there
for experimenal purposes, he drew off
some blood samples. Then, with the
aid of his assistant, he took the blood
samples he had obtained from
us. The monkey’s blood, under the
microscope, seemed full of rather
elongated, wriggling germs, of a pe
culiar species. In and out they made
their way among the blood corpuscles,
each like a dart aimed at life itself.
Then he took the samples of our
blood. In them were the same germs
—carried by that gruesome tick!
“The spirillum!” he muttered. “They
are infected with African recurrent
fever. The only remedy is atoxyl, ad
ministered intravenously, after the
manner of Professor Ehrlich’s famous
‘606.’ ”
Godowski had rung the call box has
tily for a messenger, when Long Sin,
who had managed stealthily to creep
up to the doctor’s laboratory window,
scowled through at the action —then
moved away.
While his assistant gathered the ap
paratus, the doctor wrote:
Miss Anne Septix,
301 V/. th St.:
Please go at once to the apartment
of Craig Kennedy, Claremont
Ave. Surgical case.
GODOWSKI, M. D.
The boy arrived finally and the doc
tor gave him a generous tip, to hurry
with the note.
He had turned the corner, however,
when Long Sin appeared. Subtly he
played on the boy’s cupidity to get
him to deliver a note of his own —
even offered to deliver the boy’s note
for him. The flash of a five dollar bill
made the rest easy.
As the boy disappeared on a fake
errand, Long Sin, with the real note,
hurried downtown, smiling wickedly.
“They have discovered the fever,
master,” he reported in the den.
Wu was beside himself with rage.
Before he could speak, however, Long
Sin spread out Godowski’s message.
“But I have this,” he added.
It took merely a glance to suggest
to Wu anew plan of action. He rose
and moved quickly into the back room.
“Come,” he ordered Weepy Mary.
“You munt dress up aa a nurse—im
med!?tely.” Quickly she donned on*
of the numerous disguises, while Wu
planned his campaign.
“Here,” he directed when she was
ready, handing her a little vial. “You
must infect every instrument the doc
tor uses on Kennedy and Jameson—
see?”
She nodded, and a moment later was
on her way uptown.
*******
Meanwhile Godowski himself had
arrived at our apartment, much to the
relief of our friend Johnson, and was
unpacking his instruments.
Quickly he improvised two operate
ing tables and placed one of us on
each. Then, with his assistant, he put
on his white robe, mask, gloves and
other precautions for asepsis, setting
out the apparatus for the intravenous
administration of the drug that won’d
kill the spirillum.
They had finished their preparations,
and were waiting for Miss Septix.
“She ought to be here now,” muttered
Godov,'ski impatiently, looking at his
watch.
Just then a cab drove up outside.
“Perhaps that is she!” he exclaimed.
“It must be.”
A few moments later the door of
the apartment opened. His face showed
his disappointment. It was a stranger.
“Miss Septix is ill,” she introduced,
“and sent me to take her place.”
The doctor lookqd about. “Very
well, then,” he said briskly, seeing his
preparations. “Are you ready to go
ahead?”
She nodded and threw off the coat
that covered her immaculate white
uniform.
The specialist plunged whole-heart
edly into his work of saving us now.
“Hand me that needle, please,” he
directed the false nurse.
She moved over to the table near
by and took it up, pausing only long
enough to dip it secretly into a vial
she carried with her.
“Please hurry,” repeated the doctor.
She turned from the table and hand
ed it to him. He adjusted It and al
ready held it poised for the thrust
which was not to cure but to poison
us further.
“Weepy Mary!” cried a frightened
voice at our door.
# * * * * * *
Elaine had been deeply alarmed by
the sudden illness of Kennedy and the
message from Jameson. No sooner
had Kennedy gone than it flashed over
her that Wu Fang had predicted some
thing like this.
“The threat!” she exclaimed, seek
ing her cousin. “Mary, I must go to
the city—right away.”
On the next train, then, she had
been speeding back to New York.
One glance at the improvised hos
pital was enough to alarm her. But
the sight that had transfixed her was
of a woman whose face she remem
bered well, though Kennedy and I had
never seen her.
“Please, Miss,” b£gan Grodowski’s
assistant, trying to quiet Elaine, while
Godowski turned in vexation to his
work.
“No, no!” repeated Elaine. “This
woman is no nurse. She is a crim
inal!”
Godowski paused. It was true he
did not know the woman. He gazed
from Elaine to Weepy Mar* in doubt.
The game was up. Weepy Mary
dropped a piece of gauze which she
had soaked jn the solution from tho
vial which Wu had given her and bolt
ed for the door.
So sudden was her flight that no
one was quick enough to stop her.
The excitement, more than ever,
alarmed Elaine now. “Tell me,” she ap
pealed to Doctor Godowski, “what is
the matter?”
“In some way,” he replied quickly,
“they have become infected with the
bite of an African tick which carries
spirillum fever.”
Godowski raised his hands in de
spair. “I was just about to start,” he
cried. “Everything is ready. I can’t
send for another nurse. Every min
ute counts.”
Elaine had thrown off her coat and
hat. Her sleeves were up in a mo
ment, and before the doctor knew what
she was about she was scrubbing her
hands in the antiseptic wash.
“Only—show me —what to do,” she
cried. “I will be the nurse!”
Several days later, when he had re
covered sufficiently from the diaboli
cal attack that had been made upon
us, Kennedy was again at work in
the laboratory, while I was writing.
Our speaking tube sounded, and I
knew that it was Elaine and Aunt Jo
sephine.
“How do you feel?” Inquired Elaine
anxiously, as she almost ran across
the laboratory to Craig.
“Fine!” he exaggerated, brightly.
“Really?” she repeated anxiously.
“Look!” he said, turning to his mi
croscope.
He turned some blood from a test
tube in our electric incubator and
placed a drop on a slide. It wa3 some
of the blood infected by the germs car
ried by the tick.
“That is how our blood looked —be-
fore the new nurse arrived,” he smiled,
while Elaine looked at it In horror.
Then he pricked his arm and let a
drop smear on another slide.
“Now look at that —perfectly nor
mal,” he added.
“Oh, I’m so glad,” she exclaimed ra
diantly.
“Normal —thanks to you. You saved
us. You were just in time,” cried Cr&ig,
taking both her hands in his.
He was about to kiss her, when she
broke away. “Craig,” she whispered,
blushing and looking hastily at us.
Aunt Josephine and I could only
smile at the disgusted glance Craig
gave us, as he thrust his hands in
his pockets and wished us a thousand
miles away at that moment.
